+
\mathrm{GoodSubsetExtraction}
+
\mathrm{LocalDownstream}
+
\mathrm{RowMobility}_{\rm lower}
+
\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}.
}
\]

The agents also found that the universal lower-frame formulation for ordinary packets is too strong; the better target is an **actual-coefficient aggregate canonical theorem**, not a theorem for arbitrary adversarial Dirichlet packets. 

# Main synthesis

## 1. AggG1 is still the right direction, but universal lower sampling is too strong

Agent 1 refined the ordinary aggregate input.

The naive theorem:

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\ord}}\rho(m)|P(m)|^2
\ge Q^{-A}W\sum_n |b_n|^2
\]

for **all** ordinary-band packets \(P(t)=\sum b_n n^{-it}\) is probably too strong. A sampling set with gaps of size

\[
h\gg \frac{\log Q}{Q}
\]

admits bandlimited packets concentrated in the gap, making the lower frame fail.

So the better target is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual-coefficient aggregate Gate--1 for the canonical packet }X_{\can},
}
\]

not a universal sampling theorem for every possible coefficient vector.

The viable ordinary input is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\ord}}
\rho(m)|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\ord}}
\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
}
\]

This should be proved either under strong routed-center density/max-gap hypotheses, or branch-completely so regions not sampled by ordinary centers are handled by transition/singular aggregate inputs.

## 2. Good-subset extraction should use capped joint energies

Agent 2 improved the extraction layer.

Separate aggregate bounds for \(E_1\) and \(E_{23}\) do not guarantee intersection. Raw product bounds can be dominated by outliers. The robust formulation is capped product energy.

Define

\[
F_1(m)=\min\left(\frac{E_1(m)}{\eta_1},1\right),
\qquad
F_{23}(m)=\min\left(\frac{E_{23}(m)}{\eta_{23}},1\right).
\]

Then a theorem of the form

\[
\boxed{
\int F_1F_{23}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

extracts a polynomial-weight subset where both \(E_1\) and \(E_{23}\) are polynomially large.

Agent 6 extended this: downstream also needs wall/Gram/gauge goodness. So the extraction should include a third factor:

\[
F_{\rm wall}(m).
\]

The robust extraction target is therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This avoids disjoint Gate–1-good, O2/O3-good, and wall-good sets.

## 3. Aggregate O2/O3 should use a singular-value or exterior-ratio energy

Agent 3 refined the aggregate O2/O3 theorem.

The clean local quantity is:

\[
E_{23}(m)=\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2,
\]

where

\[
\mathcal A_m(c,P)
=
\left(
L_m((c+m_{\can}P)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}.
\]

A raw determinant average is dangerous because converting

\[
|\det \mathcal A_m|^2
\]

to \(\sigma_{\min}^2\) can lose a factor involving

\[
|\mathcal A_m|^{2(R+1)}.
\]

Agent 3’s better local algebraic object is the harmonic exterior ratio:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
=
\frac{\det(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)}
{e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)},
\qquad n=R+2.
}
\]

It satisfies

\[
\frac1n\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\le
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\le
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2.
\]

So the aggregate O2/O3 target can be:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m d\mu(m)\,\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Alternatively, if \(\mathcal A_m\) is normalized in a Riesz/orthogonal basis so that

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le R^C,
\]

then determinant energy becomes usable because

\[
R^{CR}=Q^{O(1)}.
\]

That gives the algebraically expandable joint target:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m
\rho(m)|T_{\can}X(m)|^2
|\det \mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m)|X(m)|^2,
}
\]

provided the operator norm is \(R^C\), not merely \(Q^C\).

## 4. Singular/transition integration is branch-complete but needs transfer

Agent 5 made the branch-complete framework precise.

Ordinary:

\[
\sum_{\mathcal C_{\ord}}\rho|T_{\can}X|^2
\ge Q^{-A}
\sum_{\mathcal C_{\ord}}\rho|X|^2.
\]

Transition:

\[
\sum_{\mathcal C_{\tr}}\rho\|\mathcal J_R^{\tr}\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum_{\mathcal C_{\tr}}\rho,
\]

with

\[
\mathcal J_R^{\tr}(m)
=
\left(
\mu^{-r}\partial_x^r
[\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)]_{x=0}
\right)_{0\le r\le R}.
\]

Singular:

\[
\sum_{\mathcal C_{\sing}}\rho\|\mathcal S_R(m)\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum_{\mathcal C_{\sing}}\rho.
\]

The singular branch’s key deterministic gap is now clear:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal S_R
\text{ must be the actual coefficient vector of a local expansion of }
\lambda^3q''_{\can}.
}
\]

If

\[
\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m_0+\lambda x)
=
\sum_{k=3}^{R+3} S_k(m_0)\Phi_k(x)+\operatorname{Rem}_R(x),
\]

then singular transfer is Green if:

\[
\int\left|\sum S_k\Phi_k\right|^2
\ge e^{-O(R\log R)}\|S\|^2
\]

and

\[
\|\operatorname{Rem}_R\|_{L^2}
\le \frac12 e^{-O(R\log R)/2}\|S\|.
\]

This is deterministic, not a new zeta arithmetic theorem, assuming the singular expansion is exact.

## 5. Good-subset downstream needs wall-goodness and row-mobility lower frame

Agent 6 found the next downstream refinement.

It is not enough to extract a subset where

\[
E_1,E_{23}\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

The subset might lie on the D4 wall or Gram-bad locus. Therefore the joint aggregate theorem must include wall energy:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\mathcal C}d\mu\,
E_1E_{23}\widetilde E_{\rm wall}
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

where

\[
\widetilde E_{\rm wall}
\]

is a capped version of

\[
|H|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)
\]

after local normalization.

Then extraction gives a subset where Gate–1, O2/O3, and D4-wall escape all hold.

After D4, the next nontrivial requirement is row mobility. Bounded overlap alone gives an upper/Bessel bound, not a lower bound. What is needed is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RowMobility}_{\rm lower}:
\quad
\sum_{\text{global rows}}\mathrm{RowEnergy}
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}
\mu(m)\mathrm{D4Energy}(m).
}
\]

This can come from a Riesz lower-frame condition or disjoint row-atom extraction.

Finally, the proof needs:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}
}
\]

to turn aggregate row energy into contradiction.

# Updated Stack C2

The corrected Stack C2 is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23,\mathrm{wall}}
+
\mathrm{GoodSubsetExtraction}
+
\mathrm{LocalGate2/D4}
+
\mathrm{RowMobility}_{\rm lower}
+
\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}.
}
\]

More explicitly, the central aggregate input should look like one of these.

## Capped energy version

\[
\boxed{
\int
F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

## Determinant/exterior version

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X(m)|^2
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\widetilde E_{\rm wall}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m
\rho(m)|X(m)|^2.
}
\]

with \(\|\mathcal A_m\|\le R^C\).

## Harmonic exterior version

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m d\mu(m)\,
E_1(m)\mathcal H_{23}(m)\widetilde E_{\rm wall}(m)
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

# Immediate next mathematical attacks

The most valuable next work is not more fork analysis. It is targeted proof attempts on these four precise statements:

\[
\boxed{
\text{1. Actual-coefficient aggregate canonical Gate--1.}
}
\]

Not universal lower frame for all packets.

\[
\boxed{
\text{2. Aggregate exterior-ratio O2/O3.}
}
\]

Prefer \(\mathcal H_{23}\) or determinant with \(R^C\)-normalized basis.

\[
\boxed{
\text{3. Joint wall-inclusive extraction theorem.}
}
\]

Prove or model

\[
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{4. RowMobility}_{\rm lower}\text{ and GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}.
}
\]

These are now the exact bottlenecks for Stack C2.

sum_{m in C_ord} rho(m)|T_can X_can(m)|^2
>= Q^{-A}
sum_{m in C_ord} rho(m)|X_can(m)|^2.

Tasks:

1. Write the exact actual-coefficient version.
   Identify what is contained in X_can:
   - plus packet;
   - folded-dual packet;
   - B_2 background;
   - D_2 dual/completion;
   - E_2 regularized tail.

   The universal lower frame fails if adversarial P(t) concentrates in gaps.
   Can the actual canonical packet concentrate in routed-center gaps?
   Use AFE structure, zero equation, and canonical transfer to test this.

3. Try an explicit-formula/zero-sampling second-moment route:
   Evaluate whether
     sum_{gamma} rho(gamma)|P_can(gamma)|^2
   can be approximated by
     (Q/2pi) int rho(t)|P_can(t)|^2 dt
   with a lower error term.
   Identify precisely where S(t) or zero-distribution errors enter.

4. Analyze ordinary-routing gaps.

     ordinary gaps are either small, or excluded mass is covered by C_tr/C_sing aggregate Gate–1.

5. Produce a final theorem target:

   B. branch-complete aggregate Gate–1 over C_ord ∪ C_tr ∪ C_sing;
   or

Deliverable:

# Agent 3 — Aggregate O2/O3 via harmonic exterior ratio

```text
You are Agent 3. Your task is to attack AggFrame_23 using the harmonic exterior ratio.

At each center m, let A_m be the augmented O2/O3 map:

A_m(c,P) = ( L_m((c + m_can P)x^k) )_{0<=k<=R+1},
deg P <= R,
n=R+2.

Define

H_23(m) =
det(A_m^* A_m) / e_{n-1}(A_m^* A_m),

with H_23=0 if A_m is singular. We have:
  (1/n) sigma_min(A_m)^2 <= H_23(m) <= sigma_min(A_m)^2.

Tasks:

1. Prove the singular-value comparison rigorously:
   H_23 = 1 / tr((A_m^*A_m)^{-1}) when invertible,
   and compare to sigma_min^2.

2. Formulate AggHarm_23:
   sum_m dmu(m) H_23(m) >= Q^{-A} mu(C).
   Show this implies aggregate sigma_min energy and then good-subset extraction.

3. Express H_23 algebraically:
   H_23 = |det A_m|^2 / sum_{i,j}|Cof_{ij}(A_m)|^2.
   Identify the exact numerator and denominator in terms of exterior powers:
     |Λ^n A_m|^2 / |Λ^{n-1} A_m|_{HS}^2.

4. Develop a dyadic denominator strategy:
   stratify by D_{n-1}(m)=e_{n-1}(A_m^*A_m).
   On D_{n-1}~Λ, reduce H_23 lower bounds to determinant-energy lower bounds.
   State what lower bound is needed on each or some dyadic slice.

5. Analyze the arithmetic difficulty:
   determinant expansions involve subset-product frequencies of length T^R.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 19:35:56)

_Thought for 14s_

Read. The responses sharpen Stack C2 substantially. The route is still viable, but the central theorem is now **joint, actual-coefficient, wall-inclusive aggregate lower sampling**, not separate aggregate Gate–1 and O2/O3 estimates. 

# Coordinator synthesis

## 1. Universal lower sampling is too strong

Agent 1’s continuation is important. A universal lower frame for **all** ordinary-band Dirichlet packets is too strong because adversarial bandlimited packets can concentrate inside routed-center gaps of size

\[
h\gg \frac{\log Q}{Q}.
\]

So the right theorem is not:

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\ord}}\rho(m)|P(m)|^2
\ge Q^{-A}W\|P\|^2
\quad\text{for all }P.
\]

The right theorem is **actual-coefficient aggregate Gate–1** for the full canonical packet:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\ord}}
\rho(m)|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\ord}}
\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
}
\]

And even better, make it branch-complete so ordinary gaps are covered by transition/singular aggregate inputs:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AggG1}_{\ord}
+
\mathrm{AggG1}_{\tr}
+
\mathrm{AggG1}_{\sing}.
}
\]

The missing arithmetic theorem is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch-complete zero-sampling second moment for the actual canonical packet }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

## 2. Extraction is Green if capped and joint

Agent 2’s extraction layer is essentially solved.

Define capped factors

\[
F_1=\min(E_1/\eta_1,1),
\qquad
F_{23}=\min(E_{23}/\eta_{23},1),
\qquad
F_{\rm wall}=\min(E_{\rm wall}/\eta_{\rm wall},1).
\]

If

\[
\boxed{
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
}
\]

then there is a polynomial-weight subset \(\mathcal G\) where all three local conditions hold:

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A'},\qquad
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A'},\qquad
E_{\rm wall}(m)\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

This avoids disjoint-good-set problems and outlier domination. Extraction itself is no longer the blocker.

## 3. Aggregate O2/O3 should use \(\mathcal H_{23}\)

Agent 3’s harmonic exterior ratio is the correct local quantity:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
=
\frac{\det(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)}
{e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)},
\qquad n=R+2.
}
\]

It satisfies

\[
\frac1n\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\le
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\le
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2.
\]

So the aggregate O2/O3 target is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m d\mu(m)\,\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Equivalently, use determinant energy only if the augmented matrix is represented in a basis with

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le R^C,
\]

because then determinant-to-\(\sigma_{\min}\) loses only

\[
R^{CR}=Q^{O(1)}.
\]

Without \(R^C\)-normalization, raw determinant energy is unsafe.

## 4. The central joint theorem now has three factors

Agent 4’s best formulation is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m d\mu(m)\,
E_1(m)\,
\mathcal H_{23}(m)\,
\widetilde E_{\rm wall}(m)
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is the clean Stack C2 aggregate input.

In algebraically expandable determinant form, assuming \(R^C\)-normalization:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\widetilde E_{\rm wall}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
}
\]

The remaining arithmetic theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero-restricted exterior-power lower sampling for the actual canonical packet.}
}
\]

This is a serious new theorem, but it is aggregate and dimensionally plausible, unlike the old pointwise projection theorem.

## 5. Branch-complete transfer is mostly deterministic

Agent 5 clarified the branch story.

Ordinary is already scalar-canonical.

Transition is scalar-canonical because

\[
\mathcal J_R^{\tr}(m)
=
\left(
\mu^{-r}\partial_x^r[\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)]_{x=0}
\right)_{0\le r\le R}.
\]

Singular requires a deterministic transfer lemma. If

\[
\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m_0+\lambda x)
=
\sum_{k=3}^{R+3}S_k(m_0)\Phi_k(x)
+
\operatorname{Rem}_R(x;m_0),
\]

then \(|S|\ge Q^{-A}\) implies scalar source energy if:

\[
\int\left|\sum S_k\Phi_k\right|^2
\ge e^{-O(R\log R)}|S|^2,
\]

and

\[
\|\operatorname{Rem}_R\|_{L^2}
\le
\frac12 e^{-O(R\log R)/2}|S|.
\]

So the singular branch is compatible if \(\mathcal S_R\) is the **actual coefficient vector** of the canonical local expansion, not just a diagnostic sine-moment vector.

## 6. Downstream is viable with three explicit additions

Agent 6’s downstream theorem is now exact.

Once \(\mathcal G\) has

\[
E_1,E_{23},E_{\rm wall}\ge Q^{-A},
\]

local Gate–2/D4 can run, provided \(F_{\rm wall}\) includes not only anisotropy but also:

\[
\text{Gram-good},\quad
\text{gauge-good},\quad
\text{carrier-frame-good},\quad
\text{chart-good},\quad
\text{carrier-routing-good}.
\]

Then we still need:

\[
\boxed{\mathrm{RowMobility}_{\rm lower}}
\]

not just bounded overlap. It must be a lower-frame / noncancellation theorem:

\[
\sum_{\text{rows}}\mathrm{RowEnergy}
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu(m)\|v_m\|^2.
\]

And finally:

\[
\boxed{\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}}
\]

to contradict the aggregate lower row energy.

# Corrected Stack C2

The current target architecture is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23,\mathrm{wall}}
+
\mathrm{GoodSubsetExtraction}
+
\mathrm{LocalGate2/D4}
+
\mathrm{RowMobility}_{\rm lower}
+
\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}.
}
\]

With the central input:

\[
\boxed{
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Or in exterior form:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m d\mu(m)\,
E_1(m)\,
\mathcal H_{23}(m)\,
\widetilde E_{\rm wall}(m)
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

# Current bottlenecks

The exact remaining mathematical bottlenecks are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{1. Branch-complete actual-coefficient zero-sampling second moment for }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{2. Aggregate harmonic-exterior O2/O3 lower sampling.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{3. Wall-inclusive joint aggregate lower bound.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{4. Singular coefficient-to-energy transfer.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{5. RowMobility}_{\rm lower}.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{6. GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}.
}
\]

This is a coherent proof architecture now. It is not close to closed, but the old “dimensionally impossible pointwise Gate–1” problem has been replaced by concrete aggregate lower-sampling and downstream summability theorems.

      half-leading remainder bound
        ||Rem_R||_{L2} <= 1/2 kappa_R^{1/2}|S|.

4. Audit the singular basis Phi_k.
   If Phi_k=sin(k Theta(x)) with monotone analytic Theta, prove the lower Riesz bound.
   If not, identify the precise basis-conditioning theorem needed.

5. Audit the coefficient identity:
   Is S_R the actual coefficient vector of the q''_can singular expansion?
   Or is it only a diagnostic sine-moment vector?
   State the exact identity required.

6. Branch union:
   Formulate the final BranchToCanonical theorem:
      m in G_ord ∪ G_tr ∪ G_sing
      =>
      scalar canonical Gate–1 for q''_can.

Deliverable:
A deterministic BranchToCanonical transfer theorem, or a precise list of missing identities/conditioning/remainder estimates, especially for the singular branch.
```

# Agent 6 — RowMobility lower-frame and global row-energy upper

```text
You are Agent 6. Your task is to attack the final downstream bottlenecks after joint extraction.

Assume we already have a polynomial-weight subset G where:

E_1(m), E_23(m), E_wall_full(m) >= Q^{-A}

and all local chart hypotheses hold.

Local Gate2/D4 gives local row certificates v_m in a global row-energy Hilbert space H_row.

Tasks:

1. State the local Gate2/D4 conclusion precisely:
   E_1+E_23+E_wall_full at m
   =>
   ||v_m||_{H_row}^2 >= Q^{-A} * local normalization.

2. Define the global row-energy object:
   What is RowEnergy(r)?
   What is the global Hilbert space or positive energy measure?
   What is the target row set R(G)?

3. Prove or reduce RowMobility_lower:
   Need
      sum_rows RowEnergy >= Q^{-C} sum_{m in G} mu(m)||v_m||^2.
   Compare three sufficient mechanisms:
      disjoint row-atom extraction;
      Riesz lower bound / almost orthogonality;
      positive row-energy transfer avoiding amplitude cancellation.
   Decide which is most plausible in the current architecture.

4. Bounded overlap is not enough.
   Give an explicit cancellation counterexample if only bounded overlap is known.
   State the extra sign/coherence/positivity condition needed.

5. State GlobalRowEnergyUpper_agg:
   Need
      sum_{r in R(G)} RowEnergy(r) <= upper bound
   with normalization compatible with the lower bound.
   Identify whether this upper bound exists in the current architecture or must be newly proved.

6. List final downstream failure modes:
   incomplete F_wall;
   carrier-routing hidden live terms;
   Gate2 nonuniform constants;
   row mobility cancellation;
   missing global upper bound;
   normalization mismatch;
   branch mismatch.

Deliverable:
A final downstream theorem:
   JointGoodSubset + LocalGate2/D4 + RowMobility_lower + GlobalRowEnergyUpper_agg => contradiction,
or a precise no-go at the row mobility/global upper stage.
```

_Thought for 8s_

Read. The current architecture has sharpened again. The main change is that **Stack C2 is now less vague but more conditional**: the upstream aggregate route is plausible only after replacing universal sampling by actual-coefficient/branch-complete zero-Gram analysis, and the downstream route needs an explicit forbidden identity operator \(\Lambda\). Without \(\Lambda\), D4 produces energy but not a contradiction. 

# Coordinator synthesis

## 1. AggG1 is no longer a generic lower-frame problem

Agent 1 pushed the ordinary aggregate Gate–1 input into a much more concrete operator theorem.

The old target

\[
\sum_{\gamma}\rho(\gamma)|P(\gamma)|^2
\gtrsim Q^{-A}\|P\|^2
\]

for arbitrary packets is dead. The refined target is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{macroscopic, smoothed, actual-coefficient, branch-complete zero-Gram rank-one law.}
}
\]

The important positive discovery is that, after the natural \(n^{-1/2}\) AFE normalization, the Landau/Gonek off-diagonal has leading kernel

\[
\sum_k \frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}\delta_{\log k}\approx du,
\]

so the leading zero-sampling Gram is rank-one:

\[
G_Z
\approx
I-\frac1Q e_Z\otimes e_Z.
\]

That means the main null direction is plausibly just the scalar AFE value direction, which derivative/canonical rows can see. This is a major improvement over the earlier fear of a huge aggregate nullspace.

But this works only for **smooth actual canonical packets**, not arbitrary refined-cell vectors. The noninteger-ratio terms are controlled if the height window has macroscopic length

\[
H\gg T^{c_++\delta},
\qquad n\le T^{c_+},\quad c_+<1/2.
\]

So the next AggG1 theorem should be:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Smoothed block-rank-one zero-Gram law for }
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
}
\]

The proof splits into:

\[
\boxed{\text{Arithmetic: smoothed exact-ratio zero-Gram block law}}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{\text{Deterministic: canonical symbol quotient ellipticity.}}
\]

This is now a real proof route, not just a named assumption.

## 2. Extraction is essentially solved, but the interface changed

Agent 2 closed the extraction layer in a modular way.

The clean interface is no longer “put every factor into one monolithic product.” It is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N}
+
\mathrm{GeomBadSmall}^{N}
+
\mathrm{H\text{-}from\text{-}Gate1/HSublevel}_{\nu}
+
\mathrm{GammaSublevel}_{\nu}
+
\mathrm{DyadicNorm}
+
\mathrm{GoodSubsetExtraction}.
}
\]

The key measure is often

\[
d\mu_N=N(m)\,d\rho(m),
\]

where \(N(m)\) is the branch reference energy. Dyadic normalization of \(N(m)\) solves the mismatch between unnormalized aggregate estimates and downstream \(\rho\)-weighted summation.

The wall term should be split:

\[
E_{\rm wall}
\sim
|H|^2\bigl(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2\bigr).
\]

Agent 2 found:

\[
\boxed{
E_{23}\not\Rightarrow \Gamma\text{-wall escape}
}
\]

in the abstract structure. So \(\Gamma\)-sublevel anti-concentration remains an independent input unless later D4 identities show otherwise.

The refined wall input is:

\[
\boxed{
\nu\left(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2<\varepsilon\right)
\le
Q^C\varepsilon^\beta\nu(\mathcal C),
}
\]

where

\[
d\nu=F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho.
\]

## 3. AggFrame\(_{23}\) is now a cofactor-pivot / Schur-pivot theorem

Agent 3’s harmonic exterior formulation is stable:

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
=
\frac{\det(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)}
{e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)}
\]

with

\[
\frac1n\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\le
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\le
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2.
\]

The useful reduction is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AggFrame}_{23}
\text{ reduces to finitely many scalar Schur-pivot anti-trough problems.}
}
\]

On dominant cofactor classes,

\[
\frac{\det\mathcal A_m}{\operatorname{Cof}_{ij}(\mathcal A_m)}
\]

is a Schur complement:

\[
a-v^*B^{-1}u,
\]

equivalently a residualized moment

\[
L_m(\Phi_{ij,m})+\mathcal B^{\can}_{ij,m}.
\]

This avoids moving singular vectors and recasts O2/O3 failure as:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zeros concentrate where a controlled Schur-residual moment is tiny.}
}
\]

However, fixed-test lower sampling does **not** imply this moving Schur-pivot theorem: the residual direction can rotate at prime-log speed. The exact remaining theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero-restricted moving Schur-pivot anti-trough for the actual canonical augmented matrix.}
}
\]

## 4. The determinant route exposed a new arithmetic obstruction

Agent 4 expanded the determinant via Cauchy–Binet. For the model augmented matrix,

\[
\det\mathcal A_m
=
\sum_{|S|=R+2} C_S e^{-im\log N_S},
\qquad
N_S=\prod_{n\in S}n.
\]

The coefficient has the structure

\[
C_S
=
\left(\prod_{n\in S}a_n\right)
(\det V_S)^2
\left(\prod_{n\in S}m(x_n)\right)
[x_n:n\in S]\left(\frac1m\right).
\]

This is very useful: the O3 analytic content is the divided difference

\[
[x_n:n\in S](1/m_{\can}).
\]

The analytic multiplier part may be tractable after Riesz normalization. The deeper arithmetic obstruction is collision/near-collision noncancellation:

\[
N_S=N_{S'}
\quad\text{or}\quad
|\log(N_S/N_{S'})|\lesssim T^{-1}Q^B.
\]

So the determinant route now requires:

\[
\boxed{
\text{analytic exterior multiplier mass}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{multiplicative collision / near-collision noncancellation}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{wall-weighted zero-sampling lower bound for the exterior determinant.}
}
\]

Agent 4 suggests the most promising subattack is the **phase-aligned branch route**: find a suitable branch/subpacket where exterior coefficients \(C_S\) have controlled phase, so collision classes add rather than cancel.

## 5. Singular transfer should use projected canonical source energy

Agent 5 substantially improved the branch-to-canonical transfer.

Raw singular sine moments are unsafe unless they are exactly the weighted coefficients of the actual local expansion and the omitted tail is half-leading.

The robust replacement is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal E_{\sing}(m)
=
\left|
P_R\left[
\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
\right]
\right|_{L^2(I)}^2,
}
\]

where \(P_R\) is the \(L^2\)-orthogonal projection onto the finite singular basis

\[
V_R=\operatorname{span}\{\Phi_3,\ldots,\Phi_{R+3}\}.
\]

Then transfer is automatic:

\[
\mathcal E_{\sing}(m)\le
\left\|
\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
\right\|_{L^2(I)}^2.
\]

This avoids the half-leading tail problem entirely. It also includes

\[
B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2
\]

by definition.

So the singular branch should be reformulated around **projected canonical singular energy**, not raw diagnostic sine moments, unless the raw coefficient identity and tail nonaliasing are actually proved.

## 6. The downstream contradiction now depends on a forbidden identity operator \(\Lambda\)

Agent 6’s finding is decisive.

A generic row-energy upper is useless. A lower bound of size

\[
Q^{-C}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

does not contradict an upper of size

\[
Q^C\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

The downstream needs a **forbidden row channel**:

\[
\Lambda:\mathcal H_{\rm row}\to\mathcal K
\]

such that globally

\[
|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

while local D4 certificates satisfy

\[
|\Lambda v_m|^2\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

The correct wall factor is therefore not raw anisotropy. It is the forbidden-channel D4 energy:

\[
\boxed{
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)
=
\frac{
|H(m)|^2
\left|
\Lambda E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\rm clean}(m)\Gamma(m)
\right|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\rm D4}(m)
}.
}
\]

This automatically handles rank two, rank one, and rank zero cases of the D4 live carrier’s image under \(\Lambda\).

The finite audit is now:

\[
\boxed{
M_{\rm forb}(m)=\Lambda E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\rm clean}(m).
}
\]

If \(M_{\rm forb}=0\), Stack C2 fails downstream. If rank one or two with polynomial lower image on the extracted wall condition, the downstream can work.

# Updated architecture

The corrected Stack C2 should now be written as:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N}
+
\mathrm{GeomBadSmall}^{N}
+
\mathrm{H/GammaSublevel}_{\nu}
+

+

\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

# Goodies / cross-thread interactions

## 1. Agent 1 gives the first genuinely plausible AggG1 route

The biggest positive from Agent 1 is the **rank-one Landau operator discovery**.

After the correct AFE normalization,

\[
a_n=n^{-1/2}f(\log n),
\]

the Landau/Gonek off-diagonal involves

\[
\sum_k \frac{\Lambda(k)}{k}\delta_{\log k}\approx du.
\]

That makes the leading zero-sampling Gram essentially

\[
G_Z\approx I-\frac1Q e_Z\otimes e_Z.
\]

So the main zero-sampling null direction is plausibly just the scalar AFE value direction, not a high-dimensional nullspace. That is a serious improvement over the old dimension objection.

But Agent 1’s correction is equally important: AggG1 is not about scalar packet energy. It compares canonical row energy to **cell/vector energy**:

\[
\sum_\gamma |T_{\can}X_{\can}(\gamma)|^2
\quad\text{vs.}\quad
\sum_\gamma |X_{\can}(\gamma)|^2.
\]

For refined cells of log-width

\[
\Delta\asymp \log Q,
\]

the cell denominator only sees local divisibility ratios \(k\le e^\Delta\asymp Q\). Therefore the cell-energy Gram is not killed by the global scalar zero relation. This is excellent: the denominator is stable precisely because it is local.

The refined theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Macroscopic smoothed block-rank-one AggG1 theorem.}
}
\]

It needs:

\[
H\gg T^{c_++\delta},\qquad c_+<1/2,
\]

smooth cells, actual canonical envelopes, and a deterministic symbol quotient ellipticity theorem.

### Key interaction

This makes Agent 2’s dyadic normalization natural. The reference energy

\[
N(m)=|X_{\can}(m)|^2
\]

is not arbitrary; it is exactly the stable block-cell energy Agent 1 isolated.

## 2. Agent 2’s extraction layer is now basically closed

Agent 2 has made extraction modular and robust.

The clean interface is:

\[
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N}
+
\mathrm{GeomBadSmall}^{N}
+
\mathrm{RelWall/GammaSublevel}
+
\mathrm{DyadicNorm}
\Longrightarrow
\mathrm{GoodSubset}.
\]

The key technical repair is dyadic normalization. If the aggregate estimate is naturally in the weighted measure

\[
N(m)\,d\rho(m),
\]

then first remove downstream-bad mass in that weighted measure, then choose a dyadic slice

\[
N(m)\asymp N_j.
\]

On that slice, extraction can proceed in \(\rho\)-weight while keeping local normalizations stable.

This solves a potential mismatch between upstream aggregate estimates and downstream row mobility.

### Goodie

Agent 2’s best wall decomposition is:

\[
E_{\rm wall}\sim |H|^2\left(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2\right).
\]

The \(H\)-factor may be controlled by Gate–1 plus geometric-goodness. The true independent wall input is probably only:

\[

## 3. Agent 6 supersedes the raw wall factor

The wall factor should not be

\[
|H|^2(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2)

unless the whole D4 live plane is globally forbidden.

\[
M_{\rm forb}(m)=\Lambda E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\rm clean}(m),
\]

and use the **forbidden wall energy**

\[
\boxed{
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)
=
\frac{
|H(m)|^2
|M_{\rm forb}(m)\Gamma(m)|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\rm D4}(m)
}.
}
\]

This is a major interaction: it absorbs D4-to-forbidden transversality into the extraction factor itself.

If \(M_{\rm forb}\) has rank two, raw anisotropy is fine. If it has rank one, only one linear combination of \(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\) matters. If it has rank zero, Stack C2 fails downstream.

### Most important downstream warning

A total row-energy upper bound is useless. The contradiction must be:

\[
|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

globally, but extraction plus D4 plus forbidden mobility gives

\[
|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

So the next downstream audit is not “row mobility” in the abstract. It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Identify }\Lambda
\text{ and compute }

}

This is probably the highest-priority concrete task.

## 4. Agent 3 turns O2/O3 into moving Schur-pivot anti-trough

Agent 3’s \(\mathcal H_{23}\) formulation is now the right local quantity:

\[
\mathcal H_{23}
=
\frac{\det(\mathcal A^*\mathcal A)}
{e_{n-1}(\mathcal A^*\mathcal A)}
=
\frac{1}{\operatorname{tr}((\mathcal A^*\mathcal A)^{-1})}.
\]

It avoids moving singular vectors and is comparable to

\[
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A)^2
\]

up to \(n=R+2=Q^{o(1)}\).

The next reduction is also useful:

\[
\mathcal H_{23}
\quad\leadsto\quad
\frac{D}{C_{ij}}
\]

on dominant cofactor regions. Each ratio is a Schur complement:

\[
\frac{D}{C_{ij}}=a-v^*B^{-1}u.
\]

So O2/O3 failure becomes the smallness of a **controlled residualized moment**

\[
L_m(\Phi_{ij,m})+\mathcal B_{ij,m}^{\can}.
\]

### Important no-go

Fixed-test sampling does not imply this, because \(\Phi_{ij,m}\) can move at prime-log speed. So the true theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero-restricted moving Schur-pivot anti-trough.}
}
\]

This is much sharper than “prove aggregate O2/O3.”

## 5. Agent 4 exposes a determinant arithmetic obstruction

Agent 4’s Cauchy–Binet expansion is a major structural goodie.

For the model augmented matrix,

\[
\det\mathcal A_m
=
\sum_{|S|=R+2} C_S e^{-im\log N_S},
\qquad N_S=\prod_{n\in S}n.
\]

The coefficient is

\[
C_S=
\left(\prod_{n\in S}a_n\right)
(\det V_S)^2
\left(\prod_{n\in S}m(x_n)\right)
[x_n:n\in S]\left(\frac1m\right).
\]

This isolates the analytic O3 content:

\[
[x_1,\ldots,x_{R+2}]\left(\frac1{m_{\can}}\right).
\]

So non-scalar multiplier is no longer vague. It becomes divided-difference mass of \(1/m_{\can}\).

But the hard part shifts to arithmetic:

\[
N_S=\prod_{n\in S}n
\]

has product length \(e^{RQ}\), and subset products collide or nearly collide. The determinant lower bound needs collision/near-collision noncancellation.

### Good interaction

Agent 4’s phase-aligned branch route may interact with Agent 1’s smooth subpacket/cell route. If one can choose ordinary subpackets where

\[
(1/m_{\can})^{(R+1)}
\]

has controlled phase and the main canonical branch dominates, then the exterior determinant coefficients may have aligned phases. That could neutralize multiplicative collision cancellation.

This is a concrete, potentially valuable sub-attack.

## 6. Agent 5 repairs the singular branch by changing the observable

Agent 5’s biggest contribution is to avoid the singular half-leading remainder trap.

Raw sine moments are unsafe unless they are actual weighted coefficients of the local canonical expansion and the high-mode tail is controlled.

The robust replacement is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal E_{\sing}(m)
=
\left|
P_R\left[
\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
\right]
\right|_{L^2(I)}^2.
}
\]

Here \(P_R\) is the \(L^2\)-orthogonal projection onto the finite singular basis.

Then transfer is automatic:

\[
\mathcal E_{\sing}(m)
\le
\left\|
\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
\right\|_{L^2(I)}^2.
\]

No coefficient identity, no half-leading tail, no diagnostic ambiguity. This is a strong repair.

### Key interaction

Agent 5’s projected singular energy aligns with Agent 1’s branch-complete actual-coefficient philosophy. It says: do not use proxy singular sine diagnostics; use actual canonical test-function projections. That gives one unified principle across branches:

\[
\boxed{
\text{all branch Gate–1 energies should be actual canonical projections of }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

# Places where one thread changes another

## A. Replace \(F_{\rm wall}\) by \(F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\)

Agent 2’s \(\Gamma\)-sublevel formulation is still useful, but Agent 6 shows the final wall factor should be \(\Lambda\)-dependent:

\[
F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}
=
\min(E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}/\eta,1).
\]

So the wall anti-concentration theorem should ultimately be about

\[
|M_{\rm forb}\Gamma|^2
\]

not necessarily

\[
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2.
\]

## B. Singular branch should use projected energy in Agent 1’s branch-complete theorem

Agent 1’s branch-complete theorem should no longer use

\[
|S_R|^2
\]

unless \(S_R\) is verified as actual weighted coefficients. Safer replacement:

\[
\mathcal G_{\sing}(m)
=
\left|
P_R[\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)]
\right|_{L^2(I)}^2.
\]

This removes a deterministic blocker.

## C. AggG1 macroscopic smoothing does not automatically help AggFrame\(_{23}\)

Agent 1’s exact-ratio localization works for original packet length \(n\le T^{c_+}\) with \(c_+<1/2\).

But Agent 4’s determinant exterior packet has effective length

\[
T^{O(R)}.
\]

So the macroscopic window trick does **not** automatically control O2/O3 determinant sampling. This is a critical boundary: do not over-export Agent 1’s proof route into Agent 3/4.

## D. Dyadic normalization should be built into every aggregate theorem

Agent 2’s dyadic slice mechanism should apply not only to ordinary \(N=|X_{\can}|^2\), but also branchwise:

\[
N_{\ord},\quad N_{\tr},\quad N_{\sing}.
\]

That stabilizes downstream weights and makes forbidden identity mobility easier.

# Current best theorem stack

I would now define the next working stack as:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{BlockRankOneAggG1}_{\can}
+
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{23,\mathrm{forb}}^{N}
+
\mathrm{DyadicGoodSubset}
+
\mathrm{ForbiddenD4Contradiction}.
}
\]

More explicitly:

## 1. Aggregate Gate–1

\[
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho(m)\mathcal G_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho(m)\mathcal N_b(m),
\]

with actual canonical branch energies, and singular branch using projected canonical energy.

## 2. Joint O2/O3 + forbidden wall

Use either capped form:

\[
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\,N\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A}\int N\,d\rho,
\]

or modular form:

\[
\int F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\ge Q^{-A}\int N\,d\rho
\]

plus

\[

\]

## 3. Extraction

Use Agent 2’s order:

\[
\text{remove bad mass in }FN\,d\rho
\quad\rightarrow\quad
\text{dyadic normalize }N
\quad\rightarrow\quad
\text{extract }F_1,F_{23},F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}.
\]

## 4. Downstream

Read. This round makes the state sharper: **Stack C2 is still the best architecture, but it is downstream-conditional until \(\Lambda\) is identified.** The local D4/carrier quotient is Green as a visibility theorem, but not yet as a contradiction theorem. 

# Coordinator synthesis

## 1. The biggest update: downstream is blocked on \(\Lambda\)

Agent 6 is decisive. The current draft has:

\[
\text{Gate--2}
\Rightarrow
\text{wall escape}
\Rightarrow
\text{carrier-quotient D4}
\Rightarrow
\text{row mobility}
\Rightarrow
\text{row energy}.
\]

But this only produces **positive row/carrier energy**. It does not contradict anything unless that energy lies in a globally forbidden channel.

The needed object is a global identity/residual operator

\[
\Lambda:\mathcal H_{\rm row}\to\mathcal K
\]

with

\[
|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

or ideally

\[
\Lambda V_{\rm glob}=0.
\]

Then local D4 must satisfy

\[
|\Lambda v_m|^2\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

At present:

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda\text{ is not defined in the current downstream row-energy architecture.}
}
\]

So the rank audit

\[
\operatorname{rank}\left(
\Lambda E_{\rm car}L_{\rm clean}
\big|_{\operatorname{span}\{[\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\}}
\right)
\]

cannot yet be performed.

This is now the highest-priority bottleneck. If no such \(\Lambda\) exists, Stack C2 does not close downstream, regardless of how much upstream aggregate energy is proved.

## 2. Carrier quotient D4 is Green, but only as a detector

Agent 6 classified the obvious \(\Lambda\) candidates.

Taking

\[
\Lambda=\mathrm{Id}_{\mathcal Q_{\rm car}}
\]

would have rank two, because the carrier quotient preserves

\[
[\mathsf C_7],\qquad [\mathsf C_{Y12}],
\]

and

\[
\det L_{\clean}\ne0.
\]

But this is not forbidden. It has no global upper of the form

\[
|V_{\glob}|_{\mathcal Q_{\rm car}}^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

So:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal Q_{\rm car}\text{ is a visibility quotient, not a contradiction quotient.}
}
\]

The only plausible existing forbidden-channel candidate is the final calibrated contradiction object, schematically \(\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}\). But the draft currently does not define a map

\[
\Lambda V_{\glob}=\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}
\]

nor compute

\[
\Lambda E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_7],
\qquad
\Lambda E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

That is the next finite downstream audit.

## 3. The wall factor must be \(\Lambda\)-dependent

Agent 2 correctly updates extraction around Agent 6’s finding.

The old wall factor

\[
|H|^2\left(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2\right)
\]

is only valid if the forbidden map sees the full live D4 plane.

The correct factor is:

\[
\boxed{
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)
=
\frac{
|H(m)|^2
\left|
\Lambda E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\clean}(m)\Gamma(m)
\right|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\rm D4}(m)
}.
}
\]

Equivalently, with

\[
M_{\rm forb}(m)=\Lambda E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\clean}(m),
\]

use

\[
|M_{\rm forb}(m)\Gamma(m)|^2.
\]

This automatically handles all rank cases:

\[
\operatorname{rank}M_{\rm forb}=2
\Rightarrow
\text{raw }\Gamma\text{-anisotropy works};
\]

\[
\operatorname{rank}M_{\rm forb}=1
\Rightarrow
\text{only one forbidden linear combination of }\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\text{ matters};
\]

\[
\operatorname{rank}M_{\rm forb}=0
\Rightarrow
\text{D4 misses the forbidden channel and Stack C2 fails downstream.}
\]

Agent 2’s rank-stratified extraction is the right form once \(\Lambda\) exists.

## 4. AggG1 now has a credible proof route

Agent 1’s update is positive.

For macroscopic smoothing

\[
H\gg T^{c_++\delta},
\qquad c_+<\frac12,
\]

the smoothed explicit formula localizes prime peaks to exact ratios:

\[
n=kn'
\quad\text{or}\quad
n'=kn.
\]

This kills the noninteger-ratio remainder.

For smooth cells of width

\[
\Delta\asymp \log Q,
\]

the cell denominator Gram becomes

\[
G_{\cell}
\approx
\bigoplus_a
\left(
I_a-\frac1Qe_a\otimes e_a
\right).
\]

Since

\[
|e_a|^2\asymp\Delta,
\]

the local rank-one subtraction has size

\[
\frac{\Delta}{Q}\ll1.
\]

Thus refined cell energy survives zero sampling.

The numerator has leading form

\[
G_{\can}
\approx
\sum_\ell
M_{\sigma_\ell}^*
\left(
I-\frac1Qe\otimes e
\right)
M_{\sigma_\ell}.
\]

So ordinary AggG1 reduces to a deterministic finite-symbol theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_\ell
\|\sigma_\ell f-\Pi_e(\sigma_\ell f)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_a\|f_a\|^2.
}
\]

The unresolved pieces are now precise:

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite symbol quotient ellipticity of actual }T_{\can};
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch routing preservation of the all-zero block-Gram energy.}
}
\]

This is a good state: the arithmetic part is no longer vague counter-randomness.

## 5. O2/O3 remains a hard weighted anti-trough theorem

Agent 3 confirms that the \(\Lambda\)-forbidden wall update does **not** automatically solve O2/O3.

The target is now weighted by Gate–1 and forbidden-D4 visibility:

\[
d\nu
=
F_1F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}N\,d\rho.
\]

The needed O2/O3 theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\int F_{23}\,d\nu
\ge
Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C),
}
\]

where

\[
F_{23}
=
\min\left(\mathcal H_{23}/\eta_{23},1\right).
\]

In pivot form, for each dominant cofactor region,

\[
\boxed{
\int
\min\left(
Q^A
\left|
\frac{\det \mathcal A_m}
{\operatorname{Cof}_{ij}(\mathcal A_m)}
\right|^2,
1
\right)
F_1F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}N\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-B}
\int
F_1F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}N\,d\rho.
}
\]

Equivalently, a weighted resultant bound:

\[
\int
\log_+
\frac{|\operatorname{Cof}_{ij}(\mathcal A_m)|}
{|\det\mathcal A_m|}
\,
F_1F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}N\,d\rho
\le
Q^C
\int
F_1F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}N\,d\rho.
\]

This is now the sharp O2/O3 bottleneck.

Key no-go: the Schur residual still moves at prime-log speed; fixed-test lower sampling does not imply this theorem.

## 6. Exterior determinant route: phase alignment is the best subattack

Agent 4 gives a useful but limited positive result.

Cauchy–Binet gives

\[
\det A_m
=
\sum_{|S|=N}C_Se^{-im\log N_S},
\qquad N_S=\prod_{n\in S}n,
\]

with

\[
C_S
=
\left(\prod_{n\in S}a_n\right)
(\det V_S)^2
\left(\prod_{n\in S}m(x_n)\right)
[x_n:n\in S]\left(\frac1m\right).
\]

Since

\[
(\det V_S)^2\ge0,
\]

the coefficient phase is controlled by:

\[
\prod a_n,
\qquad
\prod m(x_n),
\qquad
[x_n](1/m).
\]

The analytic O3 content is exactly the divided difference:

\[
[x_1,\ldots,x_N]\left(\frac1{m_{\can}}\right).
\]

Using Hermite–Genocchi, phase control reduces to sector control of

\[
\left(\frac1{m_{\can}}\right)^{(R+1)}.
\]

If on a subpacket these factors are sector-aligned, then exact collision classes cannot cancel.

The best route is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{phase-aligned dominant-branch exterior mass}
}
\]

with prime-labelled subsets as collision-free witnesses.

But it does **not** yet prove the full determinant lower-sampling theorem because a prime subdeterminant does not automatically certify the full augmented matrix unless the full determinant’s dominant branch is also sector-aligned.

So the concrete next theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{sectorial divided-difference mass for }1/m_{\can}
+
\text{wall-weighted zero-sampling of the resulting exterior determinant.}
}
\]

## 7. Singular branch: projected energy is safer, but may need multiresolution

Agent 5’s correction is important.

The singular proof object should not be raw sine moments. It should be actual canonical projected energy:

\[
E_{\sing}(m)
=
\left|
P_R[
\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
]
\right|_{L^2(I)}^2.
\]

This makes transfer automatic:

\[
E_{\sing}(m)\ge Q^{-A}
\Rightarrow
\|\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)\|_{L^2(I)}^2
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

But Agent 5 now points out a subtlety: a single low-mode projection \(P_R\) may miss source energy living in \(V_R^\perp\).

So either prove a low-mode capture theorem:

\[
|P_RF_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}|F_m|^2
\]

on singular packets, or use a multiresolution projected energy:

\[
\boxed{
E_{\sing}^{\multi}(m)
=
\sum_j
|P_{R,j}F_m|_{L^2(I)}^2.
}
\]

The multiresolution version is safer: every piece is still a projection of the actual scalar canonical source, so it remains compatible with downstream D4.

This is a good repair. It removes diagnostic sine-moment ambiguity and avoids a hidden low-mode projection defect.

# Current best stack

The corrected stack should now be:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{BlockRankOneAggG1}_{\can}
+
\mathrm{WeightedPivotAntiTrough}_{23}^{\Lambda}
+
\mathrm{DyadicGoodSubset}^{\Lambda}
+
\mathrm{ForbiddenD4Contradiction}^{\Lambda}.
}
\]

Where:

\[
\mathrm{BlockRankOneAggG1}_{\can}
\]

requires:

\[
\text{smoothed exact-ratio block-Gram law}
+
\text{symbol quotient ellipticity}
+
\text{branch routing}.
\]

\[
\mathrm{WeightedPivotAntiTrough}_{23}^{\Lambda}
\]

requires:

\[
\text{Gate--1/forbidden-wall weighted cofactor-pivot anti-trough}.
\]

\[
\mathrm{DyadicGoodSubset}^{\Lambda}
\]

uses:

\[
N(m)\asymp N_j,
\qquad
E_1,E_{23},E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

\[
\mathrm{ForbiddenD4Contradiction}^{\Lambda}
\]

requires:

\[
\Lambda V_{\glob}\approx0,
\qquad
\Lambda E_{\rm car}L_{\clean}\not\equiv0.
\]

# Strategic verdict

The path is still coherent, but this round makes the dependency order clear:

## First priority: identify \(\Lambda\)

Without \(\Lambda\), downstream is incomplete.

Need to construct \(\Lambda\), probably from the final calibrated \(\Xi_\zeta^{(N)}\) contradiction if that is the only available forbidden residual.

Then compute:

\[
\Lambda E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_7],
\qquad
\Lambda E_{\rm car}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

This is a finite audit.

## Second priority: finite symbol quotient ellipticity for \(T_{\can}\)

Agent 1 has reduced AggG1 to an explicit finite-symbol theorem. This should be checked directly.

## Third priority: branch routing and singular multiresolution

The singular branch should be updated to projected or multiresolution canonical energy. Then branch routing can be stated cleanly using actual canonical projections only.

## Fourth priority: weighted O2/O3 anti-trough

This remains the deepest upstream arithmetic theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{weighted exterior-pivot anti-trough under }F_1F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}N\,d\rho.
}
\]

But it is not the immediate fork. The immediate fork is whether the downstream has a real forbidden channel.

# Bottom line

This round identifies the first hard yes/no audit:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does the draft contain, or can we construct, a global identity operator }\Lambda
\text{ seen by the live D4 carriers?}
}
\]

If yes, Stack C2 remains viable and becomes finite-checkable downstream.

If no, the current proof architecture can produce aggregate local obstruction energy but has no contradiction mechanism.

and

The current Stack C2 is coherent upstream, but downstream remains conditionally blocked. 

# Main synthesis

## 1. Agent 1/5: Gate–1 should probably become local \(L^2\) source energy

The major simplification is to define branch Gate–1 directly as

\[
E_1(m)
=
\int_I
\left|
\lambda_b^3 q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_b x)
\right|^2dx.
\]

This avoids several fragile branch-specific transfers.

Old branch observables:

\[
T_{\can}X_{\can},\qquad
\mathcal J_R^{\tr},\qquad
S_R,\qquad
P_RF
\]

should become **proof tools**, not the definition of Gate–1.

If the downstream Gate–2 theorem really accepts local \(L^2\) input, this is a large cleanup:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Gate--1}=
\text{local scalar }L^2\text{ energy of }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

Then ordinary/transition/singular all feed the same scalar source

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

No raw sine diagnostics, no transition moment-null issue, no singular low-mode capture issue.

The next audit is therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does the existing Gate--2 theorem accept local }L^2\text{ source energy?}
}
\]

If yes, adopt \(L^2\)-Gate–1 globally.

If no, the finite-row \(T_{\can}\) compatibility problem returns.

## 2. Agent 1: symbol quotient ellipticity is no longer the main problem

Agent 1 corrected an important point. The \(T_{\can}\) rows are not merely finitely many scalar moments; they multiply packet functions and then sampled \(L^2\)-energy is retained.

If \(T_{\can}\) contains:

\[
C_0=1,
\]

and one genuinely nonconstant row, e.g.

\[
C_1(\mathcal D)\sim x,
\]

then symbol quotient ellipticity is essentially Green. The value row controls everything orthogonal to the scalar AFE direction \(e_Z\), and the nonconstant row detects \(e_Z\) itself:

\[
|f-\Pi_e f|^2
+
|xf-\Pi_e(xf)|^2
\ge Q^{-A}|f|^2
\]

provided

\[
\operatorname{Var}_{e_Z}(x)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

So the finite symbol audit is simple:

\[
\boxed{
C_0=1,\quad C_1\text{ nonconstant},\quad
\operatorname{Var}_{e_Z}(C_1/C_0)\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

The harder Agent 1 issues are now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{macroscopic smoothed block-rank-one zero-Gram law}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch/atlas routing or local }L^2\text{ source sampling}.
}
\]

## 3. Agent 2: extraction is Green, but cannot create contradiction

Agent 2’s extraction layer is now well understood.

Given

\[
d\nu=F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho,
\]

rank-stratify

\[
M_{\forb}(m)=\Lambda E_{\car}(m)L_{\clean}(m)
\]

by singular values

\[
s_1(m)\ge s_2(m).
\]

Rank two: raw \(\Gamma\)-anisotropy suffices.

Rank one: use the visible forbidden linear form

\[
\langle \Gamma,\ell(m)\rangle,
\]

and freeze \(\ell(m)\) by angular caps.

Rank zero: extraction cannot help; D4 is invisible to \(\Lambda\).

So extraction can produce a dyadic good subset with

\[
E_1,\ E_{23},\ E_{\wall}^{\forb}\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

But Agent 2 also found an important downstream caveat: even a coherent forbidden-channel subset can be canceled by the complement unless the global forbidden upper is either:

\[
\boxed{
\text{positive atomic}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{localizable}
}
\]

or one proves a hard dominance theorem.

Thus extraction can provide **local forbidden energy**, but not contradiction by itself.

## 4. Agent 3: weighted O2/O3 remains a real arithmetic theorem

The active O2/O3 theorem is now:

\[
\int F_{23}\,d\nu
\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C),
\]

with

\[
d\nu=F_1F_{\wall}^{\forb}N\,d\rho.
\]

On dominant cofactor classes:

\[
F_{23}
\sim
\min\left(
Q^A
\left|
\frac{\det \mathcal A_m}
{\operatorname{Cof}_{ij}(\mathcal A_m)}
\right|^2,
1
\right).
\]

Agent 3 found that ordinary analytic resultant/Jensen methods are too weak because

\[
\operatorname{type}(\det\mathcal A)\asymp QR,
\]

while zero density is only \(\asymp Q\). The determinant has enough bandwidth to interpolate the zeta zeros in a soft analytic model.

So the needed theorem must use actual coefficient structure:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual-coefficient weighted exterior-pivot anti-trough}.
}
\]

The most promising route remains Agent 4’s phase-aligned determinant approach, but it must control numerator and cofactor denominator together.

## 5. Agent 4: phase alignment is plausible analytically, hard canonically

Agent 4’s strongest positive result is:

If

\[
m_{\can}(x)\approx \kappa x^2
\]

after branch normalization, then

\[
\frac1{m_{\can}(x)}\approx \frac1{\kappa x^2},
\]

and

\[
\left(\frac1{x^2}\right)^{(R+1)}
=
(-1)^{R+1}(R+2)!x^{-R-3}
\]

has fixed phase on \(x>0\). Hermite–Genocchi then gives sectorial divided differences:

\[
[x_1,\ldots,x_{R+2}]\left(\frac1{m_{\can}}\right).
\]

So the analytic multiplier obstruction may be tractable if the actual canonical multiplier has a real elliptic \(x^2\)-core.

But the full determinant contains:

\[
P_2,\quad D_2,\quad B_2,\quad E_2,
\]

plus mixed exterior terms. A pure branch determinant is not enough unless it survives in the **full scalar canonical determinant**.

The best route is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{sectorial pure-branch mass}
+
\text{branch-count orthogonality/survival in the full determinant}.
}
\]

The key theorem is:

\[
\int |\det A_{\full}|^2\,d\nu
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int |D_{\dom}|^2\,d\nu.
\]

Without this, a prime/branch subdeterminant becomes a projected source-family certificate, not a certificate for the actual \(\mathcal A_m\).

## 6. Agent 6: finite D4 route is downstream-red unless a new \(\Lambda\) is built

This is the most decisive part of the round.

Agent 6 found:

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda\text{ is still missing.}
}
\]

More specifically:

### Optimized \(\Xi_N\)

For \(N\asymp Q\), optimized \(\Xi_N\) has the strong upper because it annihilates low orders, but that means it is blind to order \(7/9\) finite D4:

\[
\Lambda_{\Xi,N}E_{\car}L_{\clean}=0
\]

on the current live D4 plane.

### Low-\(N\) \(\Xi_4,\Xi_5\)

Low-\(N\) \(\Xi\) sees \(h_7,h_9\), so it sees finite D4. But any strong upper for it is equivalent to a new low-order forbidden identity:

\[
\Lambda_{\low}(h_7,h_9)\approx0.
\]

That identity is not currently supplied.

### One-parameter row mobility

One-dimensional row mobility gives only thickened \(L^2\) row energy. Telescoping in one parameter controls means/endpoints, not \(L^2\) energy.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{one-parameter row mobility cannot supply the needed }\Lambda.
}
\]

A finite-D4 contradiction needs a genuine two-direction row exactness/curl layer:

\[
\Lambda_{\curl}V_{\glob}=0
\]

with

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Lambda_{\curl}E_{\car}L_{\clean}
\right)\ge1.
\]

This is new downstream structure, not a small patch.

# Current strategic state

## Upstream

More promising than before.

The branch Gate–1 side may collapse to a unified theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b,m}
\rho_b(m)
\int_{I_b}
|\lambda_b^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_b x)|^2dx
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m).
}
\]

This is actual-coefficient, scalar-canonical, and branch-complete.

O2/O3 remains hard but sharply formulated as weighted exterior-pivot anti-trough.

## Downstream

Still blocked.

The finite D4 engine is a local visibility theorem. It is not yet a proof-closing contradiction because there is no identified forbidden identity channel.

The downstream choices are now exactly:

### A. Find a low-order structural \(\Lambda\)

A true identity/curl/conservation law that sees order \(7/9\) D4.

### B. Build high-order D4

Use optimized \(\Xi_N\), but then D4 must move to order

\[
2N-1\asymp Q.
\]

This is a different architecture.

### C. Use low-\(N\) \(\Xi\)

Only works if a new low-order upper is proved, which is basically option A in disguise.

# Recommended next move

The next round should focus on two audits, not more general architecture:

## 1. Gate–2 input audit

Determine whether Gate–2 truly accepts:

\[
\int_I|\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)|^2dx\ge Q^{-A}
\]

as its Gate–1 input.

If yes, redefine Gate–1 as local \(L^2\) source energy everywhere.

If no, identify the exact finite-row seed still required.

## 2. \(\Lambda_{\curl}\) / low-order identity audit

Try to construct a two-direction row exactness/curl operator:

\[
\Lambda_{\curl}
\]

with:

\[
\Lambda_{\curl}V_{\glob}=0
\]

and

\[
\operatorname{rank}
(
\Lambda_{\curl}E_{\car}L_{\clean}
)\ge1.
\]

If no such operator exists, current finite D4 cannot close the proof.

# Bottom line

The project has a cleaner upstream path now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{local scalar }L^2\text{ Gate--1}
+
\text{weighted exterior-pivot O2/O3}.
}
\]

But the downstream finite-D4 path is red until proven otherwise:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No }\Lambda,\text{ no contradiction.}
}
\]

The highest-value next mathematical task is therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{construct or refute a low-order two-direction forbidden identity }\Lambda_{\curl}.
}
\]

# 1. Major hidden simplification: Gate–1 can possibly be redefined as local scalar \(L^2\) energy

Agent 5’s singular simplification should be generalized. Instead of separate branch Gate–1 observables,

\[
T_{\can}X_{\can},\qquad 
\mathcal J_R^{\tr},\qquad 
S_R,\qquad 
P_RF,

define the branch Gate–1 energy as

\[
E_1(m)
=
\int_{I_b}
\left|
\lambda_b^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_b x)
\right|^2dx.
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{local scalar source energy for }u=q''_{\can}.
}
\]

That kills several previous obstructions at once:

\[

|\lambda_b^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_b x)|^2dx
\ge
Q^{-A}

=

}

The ordinary block-rank-one zero-Gram work then becomes a proof route for this \(L^2\) theorem, not the final definition of Gate–1.

# 2. But the key interface is Gate–2: \(L^2\)-input or finite-row input?

This is the most important unresolved audit.

There are two possibilities.

## Case A: Gate–2 accepts local \(L^2\) source energy

If the downstream theorem is really:

\int_I
|\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)|^2dx

then the upstream should be rewritten around local \(L^2\) Gate–1.

## Case B: Gate–2 needs a finite Wronskian row

If Gate–2 requires something like

\[
|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2\ge Q^{-A}N(m),
\]

then \(L^2\) source energy alone is insufficient. Multiresolution projections of \(F_m\) may show the scalar source is large, but they do not imply the specific finite row used by Gate–2/O2/O3/D4.

|F_m|_{L^2}^2\ge Q^{-A}
\Longrightarrow

}

or abandon the \(L^2\)-Gate–1 simplification.

This is probably the next single highest-value document audit.

# 3. Hidden goodie: symbol quotient ellipticity is much less scary than before

Agent 1’s correction is important. The finite \(T_{\can}\) rows are not merely scalar moments; they are pointwise multipliers whose sampled \(L^2\) energy is retained:

\[
f\mapsto \sigma_\ell f.
\]

If \(T_{\can}\) includes

\[
\sigma_0=1
\]

and one nonconstant row such as

\[
\sigma_1=x,
\]

then deterministic ellipticity is almost immediate:

\[
|f-\Pi_e f|^2
+
|xf-\Pi_e(xf)|^2
\ge Q^{-A}|f|^2
\]

provided

\[
\operatorname{Var}_{e_Z}(x)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

So the old “finite \(R\)-rows cannot see \(Q/\log Q\) cells” objection does **not** apply to this multiplier-energy model. It applied to finitely many scalar moments, not to finitely many multiplier functions with \(L^2\) sampling retained.

## Hidden opportunity

A finite audit of \(T_{\can}\) may close symbol quotient ellipticity quickly:

\[
\boxed{
C_0=1,\qquad C_1\text{ genuinely nonconstant},\qquad
\operatorname{Var}_{e_Z}(C_1/C_0)\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

If this is true, stop spending agent effort on symbol ellipticity. The bottleneck moves to the arithmetic sampling theorem and downstream \(\Lambda\).

\quad

## Hidden opportunity

Once \(\Lambda\) is identified, Agent 2’s extraction theorem can be finalized almost mechanically. The remaining extraction work is not conceptual; it is rank stratification, dyadic slicing, and anti-concentration of either \(|\Gamma|\) or \(|\ell_1\Gamma_G+\ell_2\Gamma_{G,2}|\).

So the next agent round should not ask Agent 2 for broad extraction again. Ask it to work conditionally on three possible \(\Lambda\) types:

# 5. Big downstream no-go: one-parameter row mobility cannot close

Agent 6’s no-go is serious. One-dimensional row mobility gives lower \(L^2\) energy, not contradiction. Telescoping only controls means/endpoints:

\[
\int_I A(m)\,dm=B(b)-B(a),
\]

but D4 gives:

\[
\int_I |A(m)|^2dm\ge Q^{-C}|I|.
\]

Those are compatible. A function can have large \(L^2\) norm and zero mean.

Therefore finite D4 needs one of:

\[
\boxed{
\text{a positive atomic forbidden energy upper;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{a localizable forbidden identity family;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{a genuine two-direction curl/flatness identity.}
}
\]

The most conceptually promising is the last:

\[
\Lambda_{\curl}V_{\glob}=0,
\]

with

\[
\operatorname{rank}
(\Lambda_{\curl}E_{\car}L_{\clean})\ge1.
\]

## Hidden opportunity

This reframes D4 beautifully:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 should be interpreted as a local curvature detector.}
}
\]

Then the downstream proof would be:

\[
\text{global row transport is flat}
\quad\text{but}\quad
\text{local D4 produces curvature}.
\]

That is far stronger conceptually than “row energy lower bound.” It gives a real contradiction mechanism.

The next downstream attack should therefore be:

}

m\text{-shift},\quad

\text{plus/dual branch},\quad

\]

This is a structural search, not a numerical estimate.

# 6. \(\Xi\)-based \(\Lambda\) is basically exhausted for finite D4

Agent 6’s \(\Xi\) analysis gives a clean dichotomy:

\[
\boxed{
\text{optimized }\Xi_N\text{ has the upper because it kills low orders,}

Hybrid functionals do not evade this. Either the low-order coefficient is large enough to see D4, in which case it needs a low-order upper, or it is too small to survive extraction.

# 7. Agent 3/4 hidden connection: O2/O3 may be an analytic-symbol problem plus a branch-survival problem

Agent 3 says generic resultant/Jensen arguments fail because

\[
\operatorname{type}(\det\mathcal A)\asymp QR
\]

while zero density is only \(\asymp Q\). So the determinant has enough bandwidth to interpolate zeros. Therefore the anti-trough theorem must use actual exterior coefficient structure.

Agent 4 supplies the most concrete coefficient route:

\[
C_S
=
\left(\prod a_n\right)
(\det V_S)^2
\left(\prod m(x_n)\right)
[x_n](1/m).
\]

The hidden goodie is that if

\[
m_{\can}(x)\approx\kappa x^2,
\]

then

\[
(1/m_{\can})^{(R+1)}
\]

has essentially fixed phase on a positive branch. Hermite–Genocchi then gives sectorial divided differences.

So the analytic part of O2/O3 may reduce to a finite symbol audit:

\[

m_{\can,+}(x)=\kappa x^2(1+\varepsilon(x))
}
\]

with

\[
\left(
\frac1{m_{\can,+}}-\frac1{\kappa x^2}
\right)^{(R+1)}

small relative to

\left(\frac1{\kappa x^2}\right)^{(R+1)}.

## But the scalar-canonical issue remains

A pure branch determinant does not certify the full determinant unless it survives in:

\[
\det A_{\full}.
\]

The needed theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\int |\det A_{\full}|^2d\nu
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int |D_{\dom}|^2d\nu.
}
\]

# 8. The cofactor denominator matching problem is a real new sub-bottleneck

Agent 3’s cofactor classification is a goodie.

The determinant numerator carries top divided-difference content:

\[
[S](1/m)
\quad |S|=R+2.
\]

But cofactors split:

### Type I: remove constant column

No divided difference of \(1/m\). Pure curvature block:

\[
\prod m(x_r)V_T.
\]

### Type II: remove curvature column

Interpolation coefficient of \(1/m\). Top curvature cofactor gives adjacent divided difference:

\[
[T](1/m)
\quad |T|=R+1.
\]

### Type III: row removal

Mostly basis/exterior effects.

The phase-aligned numerator only naturally matches Type II top curvature cofactors. Therefore the harmonic exterior pivot route needs a matched cofactor mass lemma:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\text{matched cofactors}}|C_{ij}|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum_{i,j}|C_{ij}|^2.
}
\]

## Hidden opportunity

This may be addressable by basis design. Use a Newton/Chebyshev basis adapted to \(1/m\) and the sectorial interval so that dominant cofactors are analytically matched.

This is a concrete finite-dimensional linear-algebra problem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Can we choose the polynomial basis so the cofactor mass lives in matched classes?}
}
\]

That is far more actionable than “prove O2/O3.”

If Gate–2 accepts \(L^2\) source energy, then the finite-row \(T_{\can}\) is no longer central to Gate–1. It becomes one proof tool for ordinary \(L^2\) source sampling.

+

+
\text{source-frame nondegeneracy}
+
\text{forbidden curvature}.

The remaining hard parts are then exactly:

\[
\boxed{
\text{weighted }L^2\text{ source lower sampling;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{weighted exterior-pivot anti-trough;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{two-direction forbidden identity } \Lambda_{\curl}.
}
\]

# 10. Hidden risk: if \(\Lambda\) is missing, upstream success becomes irrelevant

This is the harsh strategic point.

Even if you prove:

\[
\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can}),
\qquad
\mathrm{WeightedPivotAntiTrough}_{23},
\qquad

Without \(\Lambda\), D4 is just:

\[

\]

It is not contradiction.

So the downstream \(\Lambda\) audit should take precedence over expensive O2/O3 arithmetic, because it decides whether finite D4 can close at all.

# Highest-value next audits

## Audit 1 — Gate–2 input audit

Exact question:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does Gate--2 require finite row }T_{\can}X_{\can}
\text{ or only local }L^2\text{ source energy of }q''_{\can}?
}
\]

If \(L^2\) is enough, adopt \(L^2\)-Gate–1 globally.

Try to build a two-direction transport complex with:

\[
\Lambda_{\curl}V_{\glob}=0
\]

and

\[
\operatorname{rank}
(
\Lambda_{\curl}E_{\car}L_{\clean}
)\ge1.
\]

If impossible, finite D4 cannot close.

## Audit 3 — \(m_{\can}\) elliptic-symbol audit

Check whether the canonical O2/O3 multiplier satisfies:

\[
m_{\can,+}(x)=\kappa x^2(1+\varepsilon(x)).
\]

If yes, the analytic divided-difference part of O2/O3 becomes plausible.

## Audit 4 — matched cofactor basis design

Can the polynomial basis be chosen so that matched Type-II/top-curvature cofactors carry polynomial mass?

This attacks the denominator side of the exterior pivot.

## Audit 5 — branch-count survival

Can a phase-aligned pure branch determinant survive inside the full canonical determinant under the Stack C2 weight?

\[
\int |\det A_{\full}|^2d\nu
\ge Q^{-A}
\int |D_{\dom}|^2d\nu.
\]

This is the scalar-compatibility gate for the phase-aligned determinant route.

# Bottom line

The best hidden opportunity is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{replace branch Gate--1 by local scalar }L^2\text{ energy of }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

The best hidden downstream idea is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{reinterpret D4 as curvature and search for a two-direction flatness/curl identity.}
}
\]

The best hidden O2/O3 route is:

\[
\boxed{
m_{\can}\sim\kappa x^2
\Rightarrow
\text{sectorial divided differences}
\Rightarrow
\text{collision-safe exterior determinant}.
}
\]

But the most important strategic warning is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No }\Lambda,\text{ no contradiction.}
}
\]

So the project should next spend its strongest agents on **Gate–2 \(L^2\)-input verification** and **\(\Lambda_{\curl}\) construction/refutation**, before pushing further into the very hard weighted exterior-pivot arithmetic.
